attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic at eight weeks' gestation with her first pregnancy. She has a confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy on dating ultrasound scan. She has a history of antiphospholipid syndrome diagnosed two years ago following three early pregnancy losses and a deep vein thrombosis. She takes no regular medications, having ceased warfarin when planning this pregnancy. She works as an accountant and lives with her partner.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 24 kg/m2. The remainder of her examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Aspirin plus low molecular weight heparin
attributes.explanation:
This patient has antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) in pregnancy, which significantly increases the risk of both maternal thrombosis and adverse pregnancy outcomes. Her history of both venous thromboembolism and recurrent early pregnancy losses makes this a high-risk pregnancy requiring optimal anticoagulation.

Aspirin plus low molecular weight heparin is the gold standard treatment in pregnancy. The combination has been shown in multiple studies to significantly improve live birth rates compared to either agent alone. Therapeutic dosing of low molecular weight heparin is specifically indicated due to her history of deep vein thrombosis.

Aspirin alone would be inadequate for several reasons. First, it provides insufficient protection against recurrent venous thromboembolism in someone with a previous deep vein thrombosis. Second, studies have shown that combination therapy is superior to aspirin monotherapy in preventing pregnancy complications in APS.

Low molecular weight heparin alone would miss the important antiplatelet effects of aspirin, which are crucial in preventing placental complications. The synergistic effects of combination therapy are well-established in improving pregnancy outcomes in APS.

Warfarin is absolutely contraindicated during pregnancy, particularly during the first trimester (6-12 weeks gestation), due to its ability to cross the placenta. It can cause warfarin embryopathy, characterised by nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses, and central nervous system abnormalities. The risk is highest during organogenesis.

Warfarin plus aspirin combines the teratogenic risks of warfarin with inadequate anticoagulation, as warfarin takes several days to reach therapeutic levels. This would place the patient at risk of both fetal complications and thrombosis.

Management should also include regular monitoring of anti-Xa levels to ensure therapeutic anticoagulation, regular growth scans to monitor for placental insufficiency, and screening for pre-eclampsia. Anticoagulation should continue for at least six weeks postpartum, at which point warfarin can be considered if long-term anticoagulation is required.
learningPoints: Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy requires both aspirin and therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent adverse pregnancy outcomes.
Low molecular weight heparin is the anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy as it does not cross the placenta.
Women with antiphospholipid syndrome require close monitoring throughout pregnancy due to increased risk of pre-eclampsia and fetal growth restriction.
references: Bustamante JG, Goyal A, Rout P, et al. Antiphospholipid Syndrome. [Updated 2024 May 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430980/

Regan L, Rai R, Saravelos S, Li TC, Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Recurrent miscarriage: Green-top Guideline No. 17. BJOG [Internet]. 2023 Jun 19 [cited 2024 Dec 4]; Available from: https://doi.org/10.1111/1471-0528.17515.
Schreiber K, Hunt BJ. Managing antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy. Thromb Res. 2019 Sep;181 Suppl 1:S41-S46. doi: 10.1016/S0049-3848(19)30366-4. PMID: 31477227.
